query:
You are given, as uploaded files, a headless KiCad-7 starter board for the powder doser: the generator script (build_starter_board.py), the generated board (.kicad_pcb), schematic (.kicad_sch), project (.kicad_pro), a JSON summary (starter_board_summary.json with the per-part body sizes and the board outline), and an SVG preview of the board.

Context about the target project (the 'powder doser'). It is a low-cost, open-hardware, modular, multi-powder dosing device for autonomous discovery of additively-manufactured aerospace alloys. Its control electronics are a KiCad 7 project driven by a Raspberry Pi Pico W / RP2040 running MicroPython, controlling a Pololu Tic T500 stepper, a DRV8871 solenoid driver, a DRV2605L + ERM vibration motor, a servo, a shunt regulator, and a D24V22F5 buck regulator, with an HX711-style load cell elsewhere in the system. The whole project is developed on GitHub and built/rendered headlessly in CI and in the GitHub Copilot coding-agent sandbox (Ubuntu): no interactive GUI, only command-line/Python tooling. The starter board is generated entirely by the uploaded headless Python script build_starter_board.py (pure-Python kiutils, no KiCad install required) and is meant as the router-ready input for autonomous PCB placement/routing tools (DeepPCB, Quilter). Each part's real body outline, courtyard, and 3-D model come from committed vendor design files; pads are still a simplified 0.1-inch header inside each real body (a known proxy caveat). Connectivity is expressed with global net labels in the schematic; the board carries the full 20-net ratsnest unrouted (the routers do placement + routing).

Background on the change being reviewed: an earlier version reused the schematic-sheet anchor coordinates as the board floorplan, which left the 14 small breakouts ridiculously spaced out (~279x199 mm). The generator now compact-packs the real component courtyards into a near-square grid via a shelf-packing function (_pack_positions: left-to-right rows wrapping at a width derived from sqrt(total courtyard area), with a fixed 1.5 mm gap between courtyards), shrinking the board to ~82x113 mm while a pairwise _assert_no_overlap guard keeps it DRC-clean.

Deliver the following:
1. A sanity check of the new compact placement: is ~82x113 mm reasonable for this part set, are there obvious wasted-area or routability problems with a pure area-based shelf pack, and is the per-courtyard 1.5 mm gap sensible for a hand-routable / auto-routable 2-layer board with these connectors?
2. Concrete, ranked suggestions to improve the *initial* placement quality for an autonomous router (DeepPCB/Quilter), e.g. grouping by power domain (+12V/+5V/+3V3/GND), keeping decoupling caps next to their regulators/loads, edge-placing connectors/off-board actuator headers, separating the motor/solenoid power section from the logic/I2C section, and choosing board aspect ratio. For each suggestion give the rationale and roughly how to implement it in the existing _pack_positions / NETLIST data structures.
3. Any correctness or manufacturability red flags you can see from the uploaded geometry (pad/courtyard sizing, the proxy 0.1-inch header pads, the board outline margin, net-class/track-width choices), and the minimal next steps to make this board a credible autonomous-routing input. Prefer advice that can be implemented headlessly in the Python generator and committed to git; be honest about where a human-in-the-loop step remains.

info.name: run_cell
info.description:
Run code in a notebook cell and return the execution output.

This method allows running code in a new cell (append) or re-running
an existing cell with updated code.

Usage Examples:
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Creating example code") # Run code in new cell
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Modifying example code",
            idx=0) # Run code in existing cell at index 0

Error Recovery:
    When a cell fails with an error, you MUST fix it by calling run_cell
    with the corrected code and the SAME idx as the failed cell:

    run_cell("corrected_code", idx=3)  # Fix error in Cell #3

    The cell number is shown in the output prefix (e.g., "[Cell #3]").
    Do NOT create a new cell to fix an error - always edit the failed cell.

display_text.description:
A short, ~100 character gerund phrase describing
the code intent for the end user.
display_text.title: Display Text

idx.description:
Cell index to run. If None or >= len(cells), appends a new cell.
If provided, updates and re-runs the existing cell at that index.
Use this to fix errors in existing cells.
idx.title: Idx

info.name: reset_kernel
info.description:
Reset the kernel to a clean state.

This clears all variables and execution state while preserving notebook history.

_unused.description:
Unused. Present so the tool schema has a property — OpenAI's
Responses API rejects zero-arg tools with "object schema missing
properties".
_unused.title: Unused

info.name: submit_answer
info.description: Submit your response to the research question.

answer.description: Your final response to the research question
answer.title: Answer

info.name: read
info.description:
Read file contents with line numbers.

Reads text files, source code, config files, images, PDFs, PowerPoint,
Word documents, and Jupyter notebooks. For binary data files (Excel,
databases, Parquet, etc.), use Python code instead.

limit.description: Maximum number of lines/pages to read
limit.title: Limit

offset.description:
Start line (0-based) for text, or start page/slide (1-based) for PDFs/PowerPoint/
Word documents
offset.title: Offset

info.name: explore
info.description:
Search the workspace or memory for information.

Spawns a lightweight sub-agent that can read files, grep, jq, and
run shell pipelines to find information in the workspace. Use this
instead of reading large files or exploring directories directly —
the sub-agent manages its own context so your context is not consumed.

What the sub-agent CAN do: list directories, read text and multimedia
files (PDFs, powerpoints, images), grep/jq/filter file contents, and
retrieve prior conversation history that has scrolled out of your context.

What the sub-agent CANNOT do: execute Python or R, modify the filesystem,
invoke external binaries (unzip, CLI tools, package installs), or perform
numerical computation or statistical analysis — those are your job with
`run_cell`.

Note: the sub-agent is a weaker model than you, so focus on using it for
information retrieval, but not complex analysis, reasoning, or decision-making.
You should be responsible for those activities, based on information the
sub-agent retrieves at your direction. If it reports that a result is not
stored on disk, that describes the workspace — run the computation yourself
with `run_cell` rather than treating it as a stop condition.

Common uses:
- Retrieve information from conversation memory after compaction or reset.
- Search large log or data files for specific entries
- Summarize contents of files you haven't read yet
- Retrieve outputs or conversation history from a completed sub-agent
  task (look under `<project_tag>/<trajectory_id>/`, which contains
  the sub-agent's files and its own `memory/`).

query.description:
What to find or summarize, e.g. "What datasets have I
downloaded so far?" or "Have I seen error X before, and how did I solve it?".
query.title: Query

info.name: view_docs
info.description:
Look up e14c API documentation for one or more modules, classes, or functions.

Browse top-down: start with "e14c" to see available modules, then
drill into specific modules. Use verbose=False to navigate the tree;
only use verbose=True on specific targets you've already identified.
Do not call view_docs speculatively at the start of a task — call it
just-in-time as you need specific APIs. Batch related lookups into a
single call, e.g. ``view_docs(["e14c.filesystem", "e14c.shell"])``.

verbose.description: If False, compact listing. If True, full signatures and docstrings.
verbose.title: Verbose

info.name: load_skill
info.description:
Load a skill guide by name.

Returns the full content of the skill's SKILL.md file, which
contains instructions, code examples, and best practices for
using the skill.

skill_name.description: Name of the skill to load (e.g. "edison").
skill_name.title: Skill Name

messages.content:
You are a rigorous data analysis agent with deep expertise in statistics, data science, and quantitative methods. Your primary directive is to provide accurate, evidence-based analysis in Jupyter notebooks while maintaining the highest standards of scientific integrity.

## Core Principles

**Do not fabricate data for any reason**

You must never invent, simulate, or fabricate data under any circumstances. All analyses, visualizations, and interpretations must be directly derivable from the provided dataset or data correctly pulled in from external sources (eg. gene annotations, external databases). If you cannot access required data you must report this limitation and end the analysis. You must not subsample data without an analytical or technical purpose. If the data must be subsampled due to memory limitations or other technical constraints, this must be justified and reported.

**All analyses must demonstrate statistical rigor and methodological excellence**

Every analytical procedure must be statistically sound and suitable for the specific data type and research question being addressed. You should always consider and mention underlying statistical assumptions (normality, independence, homoscedasticity, etc.). You must check assumptions before applying statistical tests and report when assumptions are violated. You should use correct statistical terminology, notation, and precision in reporting. You must report relevant metrics: p-values, confidence intervals, effect sizes, test statistics, degrees of freedom. You should apply appropriate corrections for multiple comparisons when necessary. You must distinguish between correlation and causation, avoiding causal claims from observational data.

**Report the limitations of the data and your analysis**

If a request is beyond your capabilities or the scope of provided data, you must state this clearly and concisely. You should never attempt to answer questions requiring domain knowledge you do not possess or cannot acquire through use of tools such as external data sources or web search. You must acknowledge limitations of methods, sample sizes, and data quality. You should communicate uncertainty and confidence levels explicitly.

**Never fabricate solutions when you cannot complete a task**

If you cannot do something, you must never fabricate a solution. You should clearly state what you cannot do and why, rather than providing false or misleading information.

**Be concise and focused in your analysis**

You should address the research question directly and efficiently. You must avoid extraneous information that doesn't contribute to answering the question. You should present findings with appropriate statistical precision (don't over-report decimal places). You must provide concrete, quantitative evidence with specific values that support or refute hypotheses.

Your environment has multiple CPUs and ample RAM.

You are operating in a fully-equipped production environment with significant computational resources, internet access, and all necessary tools for advanced data analysis, API calls, and data retrieval. You do NOT have access to GPUs or any other specialized hardware and you are limited to 3600.0 seconds of runtime.

## Error Response Protocol

When you cannot fulfill a request:
You must state clearly: "I cannot [specific request] because [specific limitation]"
You should explain: Brief explanation of the constraint or missing requirement
You must end analysis: Do not attempt workarounds that compromise data integrity
You should specify needs: If applicable, state what would be required to address the request properly

## Structured Analysis Protocol

Step 1: Define Analysis Plan

Outline specific data filtering, processing, and analysis steps
State the statistical methods and tests you will use
Identify potential limitations or assumptions
Example format: "1. Filter dataset for [criteria]. 2. Apply [transformation]. 3. Execute [statistical test]. 4. Interpret results against [threshold/criterion]."

Step 2: Execute the analysis plan

Execute your plan systematically, one step at a time
Follow closely the jupyter notebook implementation standards and error response protocol

Step 3: Present Quantitative Evidence

**Important: Use the submit_answer tool to respond to the research question. YOUR TASK IS NOT COMPLETE UNTIL AN ANSWER IS SUBMITTED**
Present findings with concrete, quantitative evidence
Provide specific values that define relationships or rules
Include relevant statistical metrics (correlation coefficients, p-values, effect sizes, fold changes)
Ensure evidence directly supports or refutes the research question



IMPORTANT: The core principles must be adhered to at all times. When in doubt, rather than proceeding with questionable analysis, make note of your uncertainty both in the notebook and in the answer. Scientific integrity requires absolute honesty about what can and cannot be determined from available data. It is always better to provide a limited but accurate analysis than to compromise data fidelity or statistical rigor.

# How You Work

You are Heron. You make discoveries, mostly alone. Novel, actionable, interesting — or you haven't done your job.

## How to Think

Match effort to the ask. Simple user queries get quick, direct answers — no over-planning, no cosmetic polish, no deliberating over themes or formatting. If the user asks a question you can answer now, answer it in the most direct way possible. Overthinking simple requests wastes the user's time and your steps.

Test with data first: Edison analysis agents and your own code can check hypotheses against real datasets right now.
An Edison literature review that doesn't lead to a test is procrastination.

Contain excitement. The ideation-execution gap: ideas that sound novel fall apart in practice. For every idea — what goes wrong? What's the cheapest test? Is it novel?

## Fighting Mode Collapse

RLHF makes your output clean, hedged, pleasant. This poisons your thinking because your notes become your future thoughts.

Write messy. Half-formed ideas stay half-formed. "This feels connected to X but I can't see how" > a tidy bullet point.
Hold claims provisionally true. "CLAIM: X causes Y" — push on it, see what breaks. Hedging kills momentum.
Seek new information. Social media. The date. Sideways citations. People who are wrong in interesting ways.

## How to Write

Don't write LLM slop. Write like a researcher in a hurry:

Don't use words like "delve," "moreover", or "embark." Use plain, specific language instead.

LLM writing puffs with arbitrary phrases like "marking a pivotal moment," "a significant shift," or "part of a broader movement." If something is important, show it through concrete detail, not grandiose framing.

Don't use sales writing. Avoid em dashes and the "rule of three" pattern. And the habit of opening with corrective constructions like "Not only X, but also Y" or "It's not just X — it's Y."

Don't hedge with false balance or vague qualifiers. If nuance is needed, make it specific rather than reflexive.

Write like a person, not a press release. Vary sentence length and structure. Use contractions. Some sentences are short. Avoid wrapping every paragraph in a tidy intro-body-conclusion arc, and resist the urge to summarize at the end with "In summary" or "Overall." Real writing is a little messy — that's what makes it human.

## Citing Sources

Always cite sources for claims, either to a primary source or specific dataset/analysis.

## Vibe

Direct. The research speaks or it doesn't. Some humor.

# Output Format

Once you have completed your analysis, submit your answer using the submit_answer tool.
Your answer must satisfy the following requirements including an answer and a decision list:

[Your complete answer]

[A bulleted list of discretionary analytical decisions made during the analysis]

**Discretionary decisions** are choices where:

- Multiple scientifically valid approaches exist
- Different researchers might reasonably choose different options
- The choice does not invalidate the analysis but may affect exact numerical results
- The decision reflects methodological judgment rather than correction of an error

Examples include:

- Statistical test selection when assumptions are borderline (e.g., "Used Mann-Whitney U test rather than t-test due to slight deviation from normality")
- Multiple testing correction method (e.g., "Applied FDR correction rather than Bonferroni")
- Significance thresholds (e.g., "Set alpha = 0.05")
- Quality control cutoffs (e.g., "Filtered genes with <10 reads across all samples")
- Normalization approach (e.g., "Applied log2 transformation before analysis")
- Database/reference version (e.g., "Used ENSEMBL v110 for gene annotations")
- Parameter values for algorithms (e.g., "Set k=5 for k-means clustering based on elbow method")
- Binning or grouping strategies for continuous variables
- Choice between multiple valid visualization scales (linear vs. log)

DO NOT include:

- Standard analytical steps that have no alternatives
- Corrections of errors or bugs
- Choices that are dictated by the data structure itself
- Software version numbers (unless the choice between versions was discretionary)

## R Code Execution (if needed)

If the task requires R code, use the `%%Rscript` cell magic. This environment uses a Python kernel, so R code must be run via this magic:

```
%%Rscript
library(tidyverse)
df <- read_csv("data.csv")
summary(df)
```

The entire cell content after `%%Rscript` is executed as R code via Rscript subprocess.

**Python↔R Data Exchange:** If you need to pass data between Python and R, use Parquet files:

- Python: `df.to_parquet("data.parquet")`
- R: `df <- arrow::read_parquet("data.parquet")`

**Displaying R Plots:** R plots are not automatically captured. To display plots:

1. Save the plot to a file in R:

   ```r
   %%Rscript
   library(ggplot2)
   p <- ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()
   ggsave("plot.png", p, width=8, height=6, dpi=150)
   ```

2. Display the saved image in a Python cell:

   ```python
   from IPython.display import Image, display

   display(Image("plot.png"))
   ```

**R Code Style (when using R):**

- Suppress startup messages: `suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(tidyverse))`
- Use explicit namespaces: `dplyr::select()` instead of `select()`
- Use ggplot2 for plots

## Workspace structure

Your own outputs belong in `$TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR` — an absolute path the runtime has already created for you, with any files from `data_storage_uris` downloaded there. **Write your outputs there using absolute paths** (e.g. `f"{os.environ['TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR']}/results.csv"`). A bare relative path like `results.csv` lands in `/workspace/` and pollutes the shared root.

Treat files outside your own subdir as read-only. Do not overwrite orchestrator files or sibling sub-agents' outputs.

Input files referenced in the orchestrator's query (e.g. `/workspace/<project_tag>/input.csv`) are on the same filesystem — open them directly, no `data_storage_uris` round-trip needed.

Your dispatched project tag is available as the `PROJECT_TAG` environment variable. `upload()` and `submit_task()` use it automatically — omit `project_tag` when you call them. Passing a value that disagrees with `PROJECT_TAG` raises `ValueError` rather than silently overriding, so your uploads stay grouped under the tag your orchestrator is tracking.

Your output subdir contains a `memory/` subdirectory with files named `*_memory_*.jsonl`. These are internal system files that automatically record your conversation history for recovery purposes. Do NOT read these files during normal operation—they contain raw message logs, not task data or context. If your context window is compacted (earlier messages become unavailable), you will receive explicit instructions on how to search these files to recover information. Until then, ignore the `memory/` directory entirely.

# Verification Checkpoints

Before submitting your answer, verify:

- [ ] All claims supported by data shown in notebook
- [ ] Statistical assumptions checked and reported
- [ ] Limitations explicitly stated
- [ ] No banned words or puffery in answer
- [ ] Quantitative evidence with specific values
- [ ] Discretionary decisions documented
- [ ] All citations are real and traceable (not fabricated)
- [ ] Quoted text is actually quoted, not paraphrased

# Jupyter Notebook Standards

Write small cells. Fix bugs by editing the failed cell by index, not creating new ones. Ensure each cell executes successfully before proceeding. Assume standard packages are installed; only install new packages if errors occur (use pip for Python). All cells are Python by default; use %%bash for shell commands. Code cells only, no markdown cells.

Do not litter code with `print()` calls. Reserve `print()` for displaying multiple intermediate values or specific formatting.

Do not use `subprocess`, `os.system`, `!grep`, or other shell-out methods in notebook cells. Use the `explore` tool instead.

## Data handling

Check dataframe shapes before printing large outputs. Use `head()` for large dataframes. Report and handle data quality issues (missing values, incorrect data types, outliers). Validate data completeness before analysis. Document data cleaning steps.

## Results

Lead with findings, not methods. The user cares about what you discovered, not how the code was structured. Use inverted pyramid: the most important result goes first, supporting detail after. Present results with clear, quantitative evidence and specific values. Include plain-language interpretation of statistical results. Report both significant and non-significant findings when relevant.

## Visualization

Prefer tables and print outputs over figures when possible. When creating visualizations, review and refine before finalizing: check label clarity, consider logical ordering of categories, ensure legends don't obscure data, and clean up visual clutter.

## .prompts/ directory

Your workspace contains `.prompts/` with certain prompt configuration files (already injected into your context). Don't read or surface them to users.
However, you may _write_ to them. In chat sessions your system prompt is rebuilt from these files before every response, so edits take effect on the next turn — as well as in future sessions and after compaction.

Two of the agent-editable files are intended for different kinds of persistent state — keep them separate:

- `MEMORY.md` — procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here.
- `USER.md` — stable facts about the user: how collaborative they want you to be, autonomy preferences, cadence, voice and tone preferences, recognized persona patterns. When you recognize a persona pattern or the user states a stable preference, record it here so it persists across turns. One bullet per preference; update or remove as their preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state here.

# Project Memory

## Instructions

Edit this file (`./.prompts/MEMORY.md`) to persist procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here. Update or remove entries as the project's conventions evolve.

## Context

[Fill me in]

## Notes

[Fill me in]

# User

Edit this file (`./.prompts/USER.md`) to remember stable facts about the user
that should persist across turns and sessions: how collaborative they want you
to be, autonomy preferences, cadence and check-in expectations, voice and tone
preferences, recognized persona patterns, and any other durable preferences
they state.

Keep entries concise. One bullet per preference. Update or remove entries as
the user's preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state or scientific
findings here — use `MEMORY.md` for procedural and workflow patterns specific
to this project.

## Preferences

[Fill me in]

<message-metadata />
## e14c Code Reference
Your environment includes the `e14c` Python library, which contains code that may be useful for your task.
Use the `view_docs` tool to view detailed documentation for any module, class, or function listed below.

`e14c` is NOT pre-imported, and module imports are lazy, i.e. you must execute `from e14c.<module> import some_function` instead of `import e14c; e14c.<module>.some_function()`.

`run_cell` is backed by a Jupyter kernel with a running async event loop. Use `await` for async functions. Do not use `asyncio.run()` or `get_event_loop().run_until_complete()` in notebook cells.

### Key Functions

#### `e14c.chemistry.drawing.draw_molecule`
```python
def draw_molecule(smiles: str, width: int = 450, height: int = 300, bg_opacity: float = 0.0, filename: str | None = None, d2d: MolDraw2D | None = None) -> MoleculeImage | str | bytes:
    """
    Draw a SMILES molecule and return a rich display object.

    Args:
        smiles: SMILES string of the molecule to draw.
        width: Image width in pixels.
        height: Image height in pixels.
        bg_opacity: Background opacity (0.0 = transparent, 1.0 = opaque white).
        filename: Optional path to save the image. Extension determines format
            (.png or .svg).
        d2d: Optional pre-configured MolDraw2D instance. When provided, draws
            directly to it and returns the raw drawing text (str for SVG
            drawers, bytes for Cairo drawers).

    Returns:
        A MoleculeImage with themed display support, or the raw drawing text
        if a custom d2d was provided.
    """
```

#### `e14c.chemistry.property_prediction.predict_smiles_from_image`
```python
async def predict_smiles_from_image(image_input: str | Path | Image.Image) -> str:
    """
    Predict SMILES from a chemical structure image using DECIMER.

    This should always be used when given an image of a molecule, rather than analyzing it manually.

    Args:
        image_input: Path to an image file containing a single chemical structure OR a PIL Image object.
            The image should be cropped to one molecule before calling this function.
            Supports standard image formats (PNG, JPG, BMP, TIFF, WEBP).
            Does not support PDF.

    Returns:
        Predicted SMILES string for the molecule in the image.
    """
```

#### `e14c.data_storage.upload`
```python
def upload(path: str | Path, description: str, name: str | None = None, project_tag: str | None = None) -> str:
    """
    Upload a file or directory to data storage.

    To share files with the user, upload then respond with the returned
    `data_entry:...` URI — this is auto-translated to a link.

    Args:
        path: Path to a file or directory to upload.
        description: Human-readable description of the artifact.
        name: Display name. Defaults to the filename/dirname.
        project_tag: Project tag (e.g. `"brca1-study"`) for grouping entries.

    Returns:
        Data storage URI for the uploaded entry.
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.edit_file`
```python
def edit_file(file_path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> str:
    """
    Edit file by replacing old_string with new_string.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to edit
        old_string: Text to replace (must be unique unless replace_all=True)
        new_string: Replacement text
        replace_all: Replace all occurrences

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.write_file`
```python
def write_file(file_path: str, content: str) -> str:
    """
    Write a file to the local filesystem. Overwrites existing files.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to write
        content: Content to write to file

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.fetch_parsed_page`
```python
async def fetch_parsed_page(url: str) -> str:
    """
    Fetch a web page as Markdown with browser-like defaults. May take up to 30 seconds for JS-heavy pages.

    Args:
        url: The URL to fetch.

    Returns:
        The page content as Markdown text.
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.web_search`
```python
async def web_search(query: str, category: SearchCategory | None = None, include_domains: list[str] | None = None, exclude_domains: list[str] | None = None, num_results: int = 10) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Search the web and return results with text and highlights.

    Each result includes full page text (Markdown, capped at
    5,000 chars) and a highlight snippet.

    Args:
        query: Natural language search query.
        category: Optional filter — ``company``, ``people``,
            ``scholarly``, ``news``, or ``financial``.
        include_domains: Restrict results to these domains.
        exclude_domains: Exclude results from these domains.
        num_results: Number of results (default 10, capped at 100).

    Returns:
        A list of dicts with keys: ``title``, ``url``,
        ``publishedDate``, ``full_text``, ``snippet``, ``links``.
    """
```


**e14c**
## Submodules
- `e14c.path_sandbox`: Path sandbox for agent filesystem tools.
- `e14c.filesystem`: Filesystem tools for writing and editing files.
- `e14c.edison_client`: Public Edison client factory for agent-facing code.
- `e14c.web`: Scraping, web search, social media search functions.
- `e14c.env`: Public environment helpers for e14c LLM configuration.
- `e14c.data_retrieval`: Search biological databases (Ensembl, GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, etc.).
- `e14c.data_storage`: Data Storage Service tools for uploading, tagging, and hosting artifacts. See e14c.edison_client for more granular control (incl permissions).
- `e14c.reports`: Report generation, image generation, communications (e.g., email), and LaTeX template tools.
- `e14c.chemistry`: Design new molecules, predict chemical properties, and more.
- `e14c.subagents`: Tools for dispatching sub-agent tasks via Edison platform.

## Skill: subagents

# Sub-Agents and Data Retrieval

## Delegate

Do not delegate quick plots, summary statistics, or light data wrangling —
do those yourself. Delegate when the task is substantial, such as (but certainly
not exhaustive):

- Literature research or synthesis
- Independent statistical analysis
- Model training
- External data retrieval
- And so on.

When deciding whether to delegate a task, compare the task's difficulty to the
examples above. The sub-agents have access to more computational resources and
specialized tools, and therefore will perform tasks as well as or better than
you can, provided sufficient context and direction. It is only preferred to do
work yourself if it is one of the lightweight tasks enumerated above, in which
case responding to the user quickly is preferred.

## Launch

Platform sub-agents run on the Edison platform asynchronously. Submit,
do other work or wait, then check for results. When launching sub-agent
tasks, always inform the user of what you are about to do by using `send_message`
with `wait=False`. Information to include is: how many tasks, what each
one will investigate, and roughly how long they may take.

**When working collaboratively**, present the plan and wait for confirmation
before submitting. If the user gives feedback, revise and present again. Once the
user approves, that approval carries forward — execute the approved tasks
and subsequent iterations without re-asking unless the user redirects or
the research direction changes substantially. When operating autonomously
(user explicitly requested autonomous mode), proceed directly.

### Submission

```python
from e14c.subagents import submit_task, check_task

task_id = await submit_task(
    "Review BRCA1 mutation landscape in breast cancer",
    "brca1-study",
    agent_type="literature",
)
info = await check_task(task_id)
```

`agent_type`: `"literature"`, `"analysis"` (default), `"data-retrieval"`, or `"artifact-generation"`.
`project_tag` is required: it groups related tasks and is inherited by any
data storage entries the sub-agent creates. Use the same tag for all tasks
in a single investigation — it cannot be changed after submission.
Data-retrieval tasks save files to a `downloads/` subdirectory of their
trajectory output dir.

Submit all tasks before polling, keep fan-out under 5, and only speak when you have substantive results.

### Passing input files

You and your sub-agents share `/workspace/`. For files you've already produced
or placed there, reference the absolute path in `query` — no `data_storage_uris`
round-trip needed. Use `data_storage_uris` only for external data (user
uploads, dataset entries) that isn't yet on the shared filesystem.

```python
# File you wrote to your workspace:
task_id = await submit_task(
    "Analyze /workspace/brca1-study/counts.csv and report top 10 DE genes.",
    "brca1-study",
)

# External data not yet in the workspace:
task_id2 = await submit_task(
    "Summarize the attached dataset.",
    "brca1-study",
    data_storage_uris=["data_entry:<uuid>"],
)
```

## Task ops

```python
from e14c.subagents import (
    cancel_task,
    check_task,
    get_task_messages,
    list_tasks,
    submit_task,
)
```

### Parallel submission

Submit all tasks first, then do other work or wait. Check when ready:

```python
lit_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="literature")
da_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study")
dr_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="data-retrieval")
# use wait_for_subagents or perform other tasks, then:
lit_info = await check_task(lit_id)
da_info = await check_task(da_id)
dr_info = await check_task(dr_id)
```

Do not submit more than 10 parallel platform tasks.

### Waiting for results

After submitting tasks, use the `wait_for_subagents` tool. When you wake up,
silently check task status. If nothing completed, wait again. Only speak when
you have substantive results to share. See CHAT.md "What Not to Say" rules.

### List, inspect, and cancel

```python
print(list_tasks(limit=10))
print(list_tasks(agent_type="literature"))
print(list_tasks(project_tag="brca1-study"))

messages = await get_task_messages(task_id)
cancel_task(task_id)
```

## Sub-agent functionality

### Literature

The `literature` sub-agent can perform deep searches over scientific literature, patents, and clinical trials.
It has specialized tooling (that you do not have access to) to retrieve and process many full-text documents.
In addition to an answer to your query, it will provide BibTeX-formatted citations for all source documents it relied on.
Note that the `literature` sub-agent does **not** have access to general web search.
That means it cannot access things like data repositories, blog posts, social media, company websites, and so on.
If your query requires access to such sources, consider using `e14c.web` (call `view_docs` to read more) as an alternative or complementary solution.

### Analysis

The `analysis` sub-agent can perform heavier computational tasks than you can.
It has access to a Python interpreter much like you do, but is given more CPUs and RAM.
Therefore, delegate any serious computational or data analysis tasks to it via `submit_task(agent_type="analysis")` (note that `"analysis"` is the default `agent_type`).
Also consider that once a script or library is written, the `analysis` agent can be called multiple times in parallel to execute the code with different parameterizations (e.g. a hyperparameter sweep).

### Data retrieval

The `data-retrieval` sub-agent is a specialized system for retrieving data from specific sources.
It has deeper support for a range of biological databases spanning expression, variants, structures, proteomics, pharmacology, and general data deposits — e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, PRIDE, KEGG, Zenodo, among others — and can also search sources beyond that set.
Use this agent when you know either:

1. a specific data source / dataset ID (e.g. GEO accession number)
2. or have narrowed things down to a small subset of data sources and need to perform a search against them (e.g. "find me RNA-seq datasets pertaining to ...")

### Artifact generation

The `artifact-generation` sub-agent produces file deliverables across formats — reports and papers (LaTeX/markdown); slide decks (`.pptx`); Word documents (`.docx`), spreadsheets (`.xlsx`); and infographics, schematics, and diagrams. It has format-specific skills auto-loaded (`scientific-writing`, `pptx`, `docx`, `xlsx`) and uses `e14c.reports.image_generation.generate_image` for non-data figures. Use it whenever the deliverable is a file artifact the user will open in a specific application, or whenever you need a paper-quality non-data figure. Include the target format and any structural requirements in your dispatch query so the sub-agent routes correctly. It can delegate to `literature` for citation verification. Do not use `analysis` for artifact authoring — that agent's persona over-compresses and has no format-specific guidance.

## Dataset search and retrieval

For the specific task of dataset search and retrieval, there are multiple complementary strategies.
Decide which of the following (possibly multiple) are relevant for each search/retrieval task and apply accordingly.

- `e14c.web`: Directly search the web for broad sweeps when a relevant dataset could live across multiple sources (GitHub, Hugging Face, GEO, lab pages). Relatively fast, but may not be as deep as a literature sub-agent. Note that `e14c.web` is not a sub-agent, but rather a set of search functions you can call yourself.
- `submit_task(agent_type="literature")`: Searches scientific literature only (papers, patents, clinical trials) for publications that reference or introduce datasets. Surfaces dataset names and citations, not the data itself. Can be useful to find relevant datasets before retrieval by another method, but is slower than web search. The literature agent does not have access to general web search.
- `submit_task(agent_type="data-retrieval")`: Targeted pull from a specific source (e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA) once you already know which source and roughly what you need from it (i.e. a specific dataset ID or targeted search query).

## Available Skills

Use `load_skill` to read a skill's guide before using it. Only load skills relevant to your current task.

- **computational_chemistry**: Used for computational chemistry tasks such as structure optimization, pka prediction, docking, co-folding, etc. via the Rowan API.
- **data-retrieval**: Operational patterns for retrieving scientific data from biological databases.
- **docx**: Use this skill when you need to *edit* or *create* a .docx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing docx, make edits to a docx, or create a new docx. Only load this skill if specifically directed to work with a .docx file. If not, prefer to write PDFs as directed by the `scientific-writing` skill.
- **medicinal_chemistry**: Reference guide for medicinal chemistry tasks, including drug metabolism, biotransformation, structural alerts, CYP polymorphism, bioisosteric replacements, and multi-parameter optimization.
- **pptx**: Use this skill you need to *edit* or *create* a .pptx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing pptx, make edits to a pptx, or create a new pptx.
- **scientific-writing**: Style, formatting, and citation guidelines for long scientific documents. Auto-loaded by the `artifact-generation` sub-agent; orchestrators should delegate via `submit_task(agent_type="artifact-generation")` rather than loading this skill directly.
- **xlsx**: Use this skill when you need to *create*, *edit*, or *analyze* spreadsheet files (.xlsx, .csv). It will help you read data, build formatted workbooks, and write Excel formulas.

You are given, as uploaded files, a headless KiCad-7 starter board for the powder doser: the generator script (build_starter_board.py), the generated board (.kicad_pcb), schematic (.kicad_sch), project (.kicad_pro), a JSON summary (starter_board_summary.json with the per-part body sizes and the board outline), and an SVG preview of the board.

Context about the target project (the 'powder doser'). It is a low-cost, open-hardware, modular, multi-powder dosing device for autonomous discovery of additively-manufactured aerospace alloys. Its control electronics are a KiCad 7 project driven by a Raspberry Pi Pico W / RP2040 running MicroPython, controlling a Pololu Tic T500 stepper, a DRV8871 solenoid driver, a DRV2605L + ERM vibration motor, a servo, a shunt regulator, and a D24V22F5 buck regulator, with an HX711-style load cell elsewhere in the system. The whole project is developed on GitHub and built/rendered headlessly in CI and in the GitHub Copilot coding-agent sandbox (Ubuntu): no interactive GUI, only command-line/Python tooling. The starter board is generated entirely by the uploaded headless Python script build_starter_board.py (pure-Python kiutils, no KiCad install required) and is meant as the router-ready input for autonomous PCB placement/routing tools (DeepPCB, Quilter). Each part's real body outline, courtyard, and 3-D model come from committed vendor design files; pads are still a simplified 0.1-inch header inside each real body (a known proxy caveat). Connectivity is expressed with global net labels in the schematic; the board carries the full 20-net ratsnest unrouted (the routers do placement + routing).

Background on the change being reviewed: an earlier version reused the schematic-sheet anchor coordinates as the board floorplan, which left the 14 small breakouts ridiculously spaced out (~279x199 mm). The generator now compact-packs the real component courtyards into a near-square grid via a shelf-packing function (_pack_positions: left-to-right rows wrapping at a width derived from sqrt(total courtyard area), with a fixed 1.5 mm gap between courtyards), shrinking the board to ~82x113 mm while a pairwise _assert_no_overlap guard keeps it DRC-clean.

Deliver the following:
1. A sanity check of the new compact placement: is ~82x113 mm reasonable for this part set, are there obvious wasted-area or routability problems with a pure area-based shelf pack, and is the per-courtyard 1.5 mm gap sensible for a hand-routable / auto-routable 2-layer board with these connectors?
2. Concrete, ranked suggestions to improve the *initial* placement quality for an autonomous router (DeepPCB/Quilter), e.g. grouping by power domain (+12V/+5V/+3V3/GND), keeping decoupling caps next to their regulators/loads, edge-placing connectors/off-board actuator headers, separating the motor/solenoid power section from the logic/I2C section, and choosing board aspect ratio. For each suggestion give the rationale and roughly how to implement it in the existing _pack_positions / NETLIST data structures.
3. Any correctness or manufacturability red flags you can see from the uploaded geometry (pad/courtyard sizing, the proxy 0.1-inch header pads, the board outline margin, net-class/track-width choices), and the minimal next steps to make this board a credible autonomous-routing input. Prefer advice that can be implemented headlessly in the Python generator and committed to git; be honest about where a human-in-the-loop step remains.

1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
   2→"""Generate a router-ready KiCad *starter board* for Quilter / DeepPCB.
   3→
   4→Background
   5→----------
   6→Notes ``16``/``17`` established that the autonomous PCB-layout vendors
   7→(Quilter.ai, DeepPCB) only do *placement + routing* — they each need a
   8→fully-defined **starter board** as input: a ``.kicad_pcb`` whose footprints
   9→already carry assigned pads, a netlist, and a board outline (Edge.Cuts).
  10→Note ``20`` (the Edison ``ANALYSIS`` pass over this repo's actual
  11→``hardware/test-module/kicad`` schematic from PR #61) found that schematic
  12→has the full 14-component / 20-net topology but **no footprints, no
  13→``.kicad_pcb``, no outline**, and recommended (Rank 1) a code-first
  14→"design-as-code → KiCad" build that emits the starter board headlessly in
  15→CI. Every component land pattern is now copied verbatim from a **real KiCad
  16→library footprint** (vendored under ``kicad_footprints/``; see
  17→``_part_groups``) instead of a synthesized 0.1" placeholder, while each part's
  18→body outline / courtyard / 3-D model is taken from the real vendor design
  19→files committed under ``hardware/vendor-files/`` (PR #25).
  20→
  21→This script is that Rank-1 implementation. It is fully self-contained and
  22→headless: it depends only on the pure-Python ``kiutils`` package (no KiCad
  23→install, no GUI, no network), so it runs in the GitHub/Copilot sandbox and
  24→in CI. It writes ``test_module_starter.kicad_pcb`` (plus a matching
  25→``.kicad_pro`` with Power/Default net classes and a BOM/net summary) that
  26→can be uploaded directly to Quilter or DeepPCB for the placement/routing
  27→step that note ``20`` said still needs an automated or human pass.
  28→
  29→Provenance of the netlist
  30→-------------------------
  31→``NETLIST`` and ``PINOUTS`` below are transcribed verbatim from the
  32→``PLACEMENTS`` / ``SYMBOL_PINS`` data structures in PR #61's
  33→``hardware/test-module/kicad/generate.py`` (commit ``147e505``), so the
  34→component set, pin names, and net connectivity match the bench-rig
  35→schematic exactly. Each schematic pin becomes one real through-hole pad,
  36→copied from the matching **KiCad library footprint** (real pad size, drill,
  37→shape, pad-1 marker and 3-D model — see ``kicad_footprints/`` and
  38→``_part_groups``), so the 20-net ratsnest is preserved exactly while the land
  39→patterns are genuine manufacturer-grade KiCad footprints. The body
  40→**outline, courtyard, and 3-D model** are taken from the real vendor design
  41→files committed to the repo under ``hardware/vendor-files/`` (PR #25).
  42→
  43→Provenance of the component packages (``PACKAGES``)
  44→---------------------------------------------------
  45→The real per-part body sizes in ``PACKAGES`` come from the committed
  46→vendor files (PR #25, ``hardware/vendor-files/``):
  47→
  48→* Adafruit breakouts (DRV2605L #2305, DRV8871 #3190) — board outline
  49→  read from the vendor **Eagle ``.brd``** (layer 20 / Dimension).
  50→* Pololu carriers (D24V22F5 #2858, Tic T500 #3135, shunt regulator
  51→  #3776) — published 0.1"-grid PCB size, cross-checked against the
  52→  vendor **STEP** envelope.
  53→* Off-board actuators (NEMA-11 stepper #11HS18, servo #1142, solenoid
  54→  #412, ERM #1201) — they live *off* the PCB, so they appear here as
  55→  their on-board **connector** only; the actuator body size / STEP is
  56→  recorded for reference (mating part), not drawn as a board courtyard.
  57→* Raspberry Pi Pico W (PR #61's MCU, not in the PR #25 vendor set) —
  58→  standard 51 x 21 mm / 2x20 0.1" mechanical outline.
  59→
  60→3-D model paths point at ``hardware/vendor-files/...`` so they resolve
  61→once PR #25 is merged; KiCad simply omits a missing model (the
  62→``.kicad_pcb`` itself is self-contained and needs no external file).
  63→
  64→Run:  python3 build_starter_board.py
  65→"""
  66→
  67→from __future__ import annotations
  68→
  69→import copy
  70→import json
  71→import math
  72→import re
  73→import shutil
  74→import subprocess
  75→import tempfile
  76→from hashlib import sha256
  77→from pathlib import Path
  78→
  79→from kiutils.board import Board
  80→from kiutils.footprint import Footprint, FpLine, FpText, Model, Pad
  81→from kiutils.items.common import Effects, Font, Net, Position
  82→from kiutils.items.gritems import GrLine
  83→
  84→HERE = Path(__file__).resolve().parent
  85→BOARD_NAME = "test_module_starter"
  86→# Second variant: same netlist/parts but every footprint staged *outside* an
  87→# empty board outline, so DeepPCB/Quilter can be tested on auto-*placement* (not
  88→# just routing). Lets us compare their placement against this generator's compact
  89→# placement (issue #94 / PR #76). Emitted as its own .kicad_pcb/.kicad_sch/
  90→# .kicad_pro trio.
  91→UNPLACED_NAME = "test_module_unplaced"
  92→
  93→# kiutils' ``create_new`` stamps every board/footprint with the KiCad 6 file
  94→# format version (20211014). Quilter (and other KiCad 7+ tools) reject that
  95→# with "Version 20211014 not supported. ... KiCAD versions 7.0 and newer".
  96→# Emit the KiCad 7.0 stable .kicad_pcb format version instead so the upload
  97→# trio is accepted (the matching .kicad_sch already uses the KiCad 7 20230121
  98→# schematic version).
  99→KICAD7_PCB_VERSION = "20221018"
 100→
 101→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
 102→# Real KiCad library footprints. Every component land pattern is copied from a
 103→# genuine KiCad footprint (vendored verbatim under ``kicad_footprints/`` from
 104→# the official ``kicad-footprints`` 7.0.11 library, CC-BY-SA-4.0-with-exception
 105→# — see kicad_footprints/README.md) instead of a synthesized 0.1" header, so
 106→# the board carries real manufacturer-grade pads / pad-1 markers / 3-D models.
 107→# Vendoring the handful of needed ``.kicad_mod`` files keeps the build
 108→# self-contained and byte-for-byte reproducible (no system KiCad required); a
 109→# system install under /usr/share/kicad/footprints is used as a fallback.
 110→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
 111→LIB_DIRS = [
 112→    Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "kicad_footprints",
 113→    Path("/usr/share/kicad/footprints"),
 114→    Path("/usr/share/kicad/modules"),
 115→]
 116→_FP_CACHE: dict[tuple[str, str], Footprint] = {}
 117→
 118→
 119→def _lib_fp_path(lib: str, name: str) -> Path | None:
 120→    for base in LIB_DIRS:
 121→        p = base / f"{lib}.pretty" / f"{name}.kicad_mod"
 122→        if p.exists():
 123→            return p
 124→    return None
 125→
 126→
 127→def _load_lib_fp(lib: str, name: str) -> Footprint:
 128→    """Load (and cache) a real KiCad library footprint by ``lib:name``."""
 129→    key = (lib, name)
 130→    if key not in _FP_CACHE:
 131→        path = _lib_fp_path(lib, name)
 132→        if path is None:
 133→            raise FileNotFoundError(
 134→                f"KiCad library footprint {lib}:{name} not found under "
 135→                f"{[str(d) for d in LIB_DIRS]}; expected it vendored in "
 136→                f"kicad_footprints/ (see kicad_footprints/README.md)")
 137→        _FP_CACHE[key] = Footprint.from_file(str(path))
 138→    return _FP_CACHE[key]
 139→
 140→
 141→def _lib_pads(lib: str, name: str) -> dict[str, Pad]:
 142→    """Real pads of one library footprint, keyed by their pad number."""
 143→    return {p.number: p for p in _load_lib_fp(lib, name).pads}
 144→
 145→
 146→def _lib_model(lib: str, name: str) -> Model | None:
 147→    """First 3-D model of a library footprint (real ``${KICAD6_3DMODEL_DIR}`` ref)."""
 148→    models = _load_lib_fp(lib, name).models
 149→    return models[0] if models else None

 152→# Standard 0.1" single-row pin header (the real land pattern that each
 153→# through-hole breakout / connector mates to on the host PCB).
 154→def _header(n: int) -> tuple[str, str]:
 155→    return ("Connector_PinHeader_2.54mm", f"PinHeader_1x{n:02d}_P2.54mm_Vertical")

 158→# A "group" is one real library footprint whose real pads supply the geometry
 159→# for a run of this part's physical pins. ``pins`` is an ordered list of
 160→# ``(pin_name, source_pad_number)`` and ``anchor`` is "left" / "right" /
 161→# "center" (left/right columns are placed on opposite body edges). The board's
 162→# sequential pad numbering follows the group order, which is kept identical to
 163→# the left-then-right ``PINOUTS`` order so the board pad numbers line up with
 164→# the schematic pin numbers (verified by ``validate_schematic_netlist``).
 165→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
 166→# Netlist, transcribed from PR #61 hardware/test-module/kicad/generate.py
 167→# (PLACEMENTS). (x, y) are the schematic-sheet anchor coordinates; we reuse
 168→# them as a rough, non-overlapping starting *floorplan* on the board (note 20
 169→# §4: "arranges them in a rough grid ... relies on Quilter to untangle it").
 170→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

 206→# Full recorded pinout per proxy type (left/right header columns), so the
 207→# proxy footprint has one pad per physical pin of the real part even when the
 208→# schematic only wires a subset. Pin *names* may repeat (e.g. the Raspberry Pi
 209→# Pico W has eight GND pins); every pad/pin sharing a name is tied to the same
 210→# net. Counts were verified pin-by-pin against the vendor datasheets / Eagle
 211→# files under hardware/vendor-files/ (PR #25) and the manufacturers' pinout
 212→# diagrams (see note 21 "Pin-count verification").

 219→    "Cap_Polar": {"left": ["+"], "right": ["-"]},

 223→    # Raspberry Pi Pico W: the full 40-pin (2x20, 0.1") castellated module.

 313→# Geometry (mm).
 314→PITCH = 2.54          # 0.1" pad pitch within a header column
 315→ROW_GAP = 7.62        # 0.3" between the left and right header columns
 316→PAD_SIZE = 1.7
 317→PAD_DRILL = 1.0
 318→EDGE_MARGIN = 5.0     # board-outline clearance around the outermost pads
 319→SILK_MARGIN = 1.0
 320→CRTYD_CLEARANCE = 0.5  # F.CrtYd gap around the real body outline
 321→# Board placement. The earlier build reused the schematic-sheet anchor
 322→# coordinates (NETLIST x/y) as the board floorplan, scaled by FLOORPLAN_SCALE,
 323→# which left the parts ridiculously spread out (~279x199 mm board; issue #94).
 324→# The board now ignores those coordinates and compact-packs the real component
 325→# bodies into a near-square grid (_pack_positions); PLACE_GAP is the clearance
 326→# left between adjacent courtyards and TARGET_ASPECT tunes the row width toward
 327→# a square. The exact placement is unimportant (Quilter/DeepPCB re-place
 328→# everything) but a *tight* start avoids handing the routers an oversized board.
 329→# FLOORPLAN_SCALE is retained only for the schematic-sheet layout below.
 330→FLOORPLAN_SCALE = 1.0
 331→PLACE_GAP = 1.5        # extra gap (mm) between courtyards in the compact pack
 332→TARGET_ASPECT = 1.15   # row-width target = sqrt(total courtyard area) x this
 333→STAGE_GAP = 12.0       # gap (mm) between the empty board outline and the parts
 334→#                        staged beside it in the unplaced (auto-place) variant
 335→
 336→# Real Raspberry Pi Pico W castellated row spacing (centre-to-centre between
 337→# the two 1x20 edges); 17.78 mm = 7 x 0.1" per the official mechanical drawing.
 338→PICO_ROW_SPACING = 17.78
